ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Lamb Lasuni Masala; mapped to 'lamb dish' (FOODON_03311737) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Lamb Lasuni Masala curry. A0CKP (cooked lamb meat) used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor; the masala/curry preparation is not captured at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific Indian lamb curry preparation. SNOMED CT does not routinely encode regional curry dishes at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Lamb Lasuni Masala is approximately 300 g, comprising tender lamb pieces in a thick garlic-spiced masala gravy, usually accompanied by rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton curry (ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017, Item: Mutton curry)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Lamb Lasuni Masala is a North Indian garlic-forward lamb curry. No exact entry exists in ICMR-NIN IFCT 2017 for this specific dish. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN 'Mutton curry' entry (per 100 g edible portion), which is the closest approved primary source match for a bone-in lamb/mutton curry cooked with Indian spices. Garlic-forward (lasuni) preparation increases garlic content but does not materially alter the macro profile relative to standard mutton curry. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, cooked' (NDB 17003) was used as a cross-reference for protein and fat values. Values are consistent across both sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-based curry dishes; analogous estimate based on lamb/mutton curry with low-GI spice-based gravy
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lamb Lasuni Masala in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 30 (low) based on the following reasoning: (1) The dish is predominantly protein and fat with a low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g per 100 g), primarily from onion, tomato, and spices. (2) The University of Sydney GI Database records meat-based curry dishes with similar macro profiles (high protein, moderate fat, low carbohydrate) as low GI, typically in the range 25–35. (3) Garlic and spices do not elevate GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 12.6) / 100 = 3.8, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 4.2 × 3 = 12.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1140 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 300 g restaurant serving of Lamb Lasuni Masala provides approximately 1140 mg sodium, representing nearly 50% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb Lasuni Masala has a low glycemic index of approximately 30 and a glycemic load of approximately 3.8 per 300 g serving, making it a suitable protein-rich main dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the high protein content (14.8 g per 100 g) supports satiety and glycemic stability.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~3.8 g per 100 g) and sodium load per serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lamb Lasuni Masala contains moderate saturated fat (~3.8 g per 100 g; ~11.4 g per 300 g serving) and high sodium per serving (~1140 mg), both of which are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150–200 g, choose lean lamb cuts, and request ghee-free or reduced-oil preparation to lower saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Lasuni Masala is a North Indian lamb curry distinguished by its bold garlic-forward flavour profile. 'Lasuni' (also spelled 'lahsuni') derives from the Hindi/Urdu word for garlic ('lahsun'), and the dish is defined by a generous tadka — a tempering technique in which whole or crushed garlic cloves are fried in hot oil or ghee until golden and fragrant before being added to the curry base. Tender bone-in or boneless lamb pieces are slow-cooked in a thick, deeply spiced masala gravy made from onions, tomatoes, ginger, garlic, cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli, and garam masala. The result is a rich, aromatic, moderately spiced curry with a savoury, slightly pungent garlic note, a tender meat texture, and a thick, clinging sauce. In Singapore, Lamb Lasuni Masala is served at North Indian restaurants, particularly those specialising in Punjabi and Mughlai cuisine, and is typically priced between SGD 19–22 per portion. It is commonly paired with naan, roti, or basmati rice. Nutritionally, the dish is a high-protein, moderate-fat food with low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic-friendly meal patterns when consumed without high-GI accompaniments. The garlic content contributes allicin, which has documented cardiovascular and antimicrobial properties. Sodium content is elevated due to salt used in the masala preparation, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-compliant.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Lamb Lasuni Masala exists in ICMR-NIN IFCT 2017 or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN 'Mutton curry' entry, which is the closest approved primary source match. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database methodology for low-carbohydrate meat curry dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN IFCT 2017 'Mutton curry' entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lamb data. No exact approved-source entry exists for Lamb Lasuni Masala specifically. 2. GI value of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 3. Sodium estimate (~380 mg per 100 g) reflects typical restaurant preparation with standard salt usage; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant. 4. The dish is served at 5 field survey data restaurants in the SGD 19.90–21.90 price range. 5. Halal status is inferred from the lamb-based, pork-free, alcohol-free nature of the dish as prepared at North Indian restaurants in Singapore; formal halal certification should be verified per restaurant. 6. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are mapped to broader ancestor classes as no specific codes exist for this dish. 7. All core macros are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a lamb curry dish; no zero-macro justification is required.
